In Transit Reconciliation Walkthrough

In Transit Walkthrough
Overview
Walkthrough Steps
Reference

In Transit Reconciliation Walkthrough

This guide walks you through reconciling the In Transit clearing account in RapidReconciler from start to finish. By the end of the process you will be able to confirm the In Transit GL balance ties to open transfer order activity, identify and resolve every source of variance, post the correct journal entries in JD Edwards, and produce a complete audit trail for period-end close.

Quick Reference — The Process at a Glance

The reconciliation is a seven-step process. Each step builds on the previous one — confirm system health first, triage open orders, then quantify and resolve each source of variance.

#StepGoal
1 Log in & verify system health Both status lights green
2 Review and triage open orders Exclude closed-but-unbalanced pairs
3 Read the Reconciliation page Identify the Out of Balance amount
4 Resolve each variance source Address every non-zero variance line
5 Investigate transaction-level variances Understand root cause of each item
6 Post journal entries and close Clear the remaining variance
7 Run integrity reports & save audit Confirm clean configuration; document the close
Key principle

RapidReconciler does not correct In Transit issues. All corrections happen in JD Edwards or through manual journal entries. RapidReconciler's job is to tell you exactly which orders are unbalanced, by how much, and why — so you can take the right action.

Where to start

If this is your first time, work through the topics in order using the sidebar — beginning with Purpose & Scope, then the Process Flow Diagram. Make sure to review Order Pairs Explained before starting the seven-step process — understanding how shipments and receipts are matched is essential to everything that follows. If you're looking up a specific step or reference, jump directly to it from the sidebar.

Purpose & Scope

What this guide covers, what is being reconciled, and the principles that govern the work.

What You Will Be Able to Do

By the end of this process, you will be able to:

  • Confirm the In Transit GL balance ties to open transfer order activity
  • Identify and resolve every source of variance
  • Post the correct journal entries in JD Edwards
  • Produce a complete audit trail for period-end close

What Is Being Reconciled

When goods are shipped from Branch A to Branch B, their value sits in a clearing account (typically via DMAAI 4220 or 4245) until they are received at the destination. If the shipment and receipt do not match in both quantity and amount, a balance remains in the clearing account that must be explained and cleared.

SideSourceDescription
In Transit BalanceST/OT order pairs (Shipments − Receipts)Open transfer order activity
GL BalanceF0902 Account BalancesPeriod-end balance for the In Transit clearing account
Out of BalanceThe difference between the twoWhat you must explain and resolve
Key principle

RapidReconciler does not correct In Transit issues. All corrections happen in JD Edwards or through manual journal entries. RapidReconciler's job is to tell you exactly which orders are unbalanced, by how much, and why — so you can take the right action.

Note on terminology

Although this guide refers to ST/OT order types, RapidReconciler detects different transfer types based on a flag in the F4211 table.

Process Flow Diagram

The reconciliation is a decision-driven process. Follow the flow below — each diamond represents a check that determines the next step. Green endpoints indicate the period is reconciled; the path through the steps is the recovery loop when variances exist.

How to read the diagram

Yellow diamonds are decision points. The path through the seven blue-numbered steps is the standard route when variances exist; the shortcut on the right (yellow diamond → Step 7) is the path when the period is already in balance.

Before You Begin

Confirm prerequisites, internalize the critical rules, and adopt the right mindset before opening the application.

Prerequisites

ItemRequirement
RapidReconciler accessLogin credentials provided by your administrator
PermissionsAccess to the In Transit module and applicable companies
JD Edwards accessRequired to investigate and correct issues identified in RapidReconciler
Background knowledgeFamiliarity with ST/OT order types, DMAAI configuration, and the In Transit clearing account

Critical Rules to Remember

Rule 1 — Status lights

Both status lights must be GREEN before making any GL adjustments.

Rule 2 — Refresh cycle

Data refreshes nightly. JD Edwards transactions entered today won't appear until tomorrow's refresh.

Rule 3 — Read-only

RapidReconciler is read-only. Every correction is made in JD Edwards.

Rule 4 — Orders page

The Orders page always shows the current position — changing the period selector does not change what it displays.

Mindset for Success

  • Do this often, not just at month-end. A weekly walk-through of the Orders page prevents period-end surprises.
  • Document as you go. Notes on transactions become the audit trail.
  • Always investigate before excluding. An exclusion you regret is harder to undo than one you delayed.

Order Pairs Explained

The order pair is the core concept behind the Orders page. The Orders page matches each shipment to its corresponding receipt and presents them as a single row — that row is the order pair. Understanding how this matching works is essential before starting the seven-step process, because exclusions, variances, and the In Transit balance itself all flow from it.

Scope

Order pairs are specific to the Orders page. The Reconciliation, Transactions, and As-Of pages each present a different view of the data and are not organized by order pair.

What Is an Order Pair?

A transfer of goods between two branches in JD Edwards is recorded as two related orders:

Shipping Side
ST Order

Transfer sales order created at the source branch when goods are shipped out.

Receiving Side
OT Order

Transfer purchase order created at the destination branch when goods are received in.

An order pair is the single, summarized view of one ST + one OT working together at the item number level. The same physical transfer of an item appears as one row in the In Transit module — not two — because both sides of the transfer are matched into a single record.

How the Pairing Works

The Orders page matches each shipment to its corresponding receipt using related order data stored on the sales order. When a transfer order is created in JD Edwards, the F4211 record carries a reference back to the originating purchase order, and RapidReconciler uses this link to bring the two sides together.

The match key

RapidReconciler pairs the ST sales order to the OT purchase order using the related order fields on F4211. The pair is then summarized at the item number level — one ST + one OT + one item = one row.

The Two Sides of an Order Pair

The diagram below summarizes how the two JD Edwards orders are joined into a single In Transit row. RapidReconciler reads each side from the underlying JDE table, then matches them using the related order data on the F4211 sales order:

AspectST Sales Order (shipping side)OT Purchase Order (receiving side)
JD Edwards table F4211 (sales order detail) F4311 / F43121 (PO detail / receipts)
Branch Source branch — where goods leave from Destination branch — where goods arrive
Records The shipment The receipt
Match key Related order fields on the F4211 record link the two sides together
Result One ST + One OT + One Item Number = One row in the In Transit module

The pair is summarized at the item number level. If a single transfer moves multiple items, each item produces its own pair — one row per ST/OT/item combination.

What an Order Pair Tells You

Once paired, RapidReconciler calculates the in-transit position for each order pair by subtracting receipts from shipments:

FieldCalculationWhat It Means
TranQtyShip Qty − Rec QtyQuantity still in transit between the two branches
TranAmtShip Amt − Rec AmtDollar value still sitting in the In Transit clearing account

When TranQty and TranAmt are both zero, the pair is fully cleared — quantity shipped equals quantity received, and the dollar value has cleared the In Transit account. Any non-zero value means there is open transfer activity that contributes to the In Transit balance.

Why This Matters for Reconciliation

  • The In Transit balance is the sum of every open pair's TranAmt. The total at the bottom of the Orders page rolls up directly into the In Transit Balance shown on the Reconciliation page.
  • Exclusions happen at the pair level. When you exclude an order in Step 2, you're telling RapidReconciler to stop counting that one ST/OT/item combination toward the In Transit balance.
  • Cost variances surface at the pair level. Integrity Report 4 examines each pair to find cases where the ST sales price doesn't equal the OT purchase cost — these will never clear without action.
  • Open pairs are the source of every variance you'll investigate. Whatever you see on the Reconciliation page can be traced back to one or more open pairs on the Orders page — which is why Step 2 (triaging the Orders page) comes before any other corrective action.
Note on terminology

Although this guide refers to ST/OT order types, RapidReconciler detects different transfer types based on a flag in the F4211 table. The pairing logic is the same regardless of the specific order type codes used in your environment.

1 Step One

Log In and Verify System Health

Goal
Confirm the system is ready for reconciliation work.

Procedure

  1. Log in to RapidReconciler at https://rapidreconciler.getgsi.com.
  2. Navigate to the In Transit module. The Reconciliation page opens by default.
  3. Use the period selector (top right) to choose the period being reconciled.
  4. Apply company and account filters as needed (Company → Business Unit → Object → Subsidiary).
  5. Verify the two status indicators:
IndicatorGreen MeansIf Red
In Transit Validation The carry-forward from prior period is accurate Hover for details; resolve the prior period before proceeding
System Status The JD Edwards import completed successfully Hover for details; contact your administrator
Yellow status

If the System Status light is flashing yellow, the import is in progress. Wait for it to finish before continuing.

Stop condition

Do not proceed to Step 2 if either light is red. Fixing the underlying issue first prevents wasted work.

2 Step Two

Review and Triage Open Orders

Goal
Make sure the Orders page lists only what is truly in transit. Everything else must be excluded.

Filter Buttons on the Orders Page

At the top of the Orders grid are three buttons that automatically filter the result set. Each one isolates a specific class of order pair that needs attention.

Receipts without Shipments

If the age of the receipt is large, this is more than likely a timing issue with the start date — the order should probably be excluded. If the date is more recent, contact the shipping branch to investigate a potential missing transaction.

Items in Transit with Closed PO

Orders where the lowest next status on any line is 999 and there is a non-zero quantity in transit. These orders need to be investigated as the quantity shipped does not agree with the quantity received.

Zero Quantity in Transit

Orders where the ship quantity matches the receipt quantity but there is an amount mismatch that must be excluded. Any exclusion amount adds to the Exclusion variance source and will be cleared during the close.

The goal

There should be no data showing for any of the three filters. Each filter that returns rows is a list of orders that need triage before the period can be reconciled cleanly.

Procedure

  1. Navigate to the Orders page.
  2. Review every order pair. For each one, decide its disposition:
SituationAction
Both ST and OT at status 999 with remaining balanceExclude — no further JD Edwards activity is possible
OT not yet received or partially received; more receipts expectedLeave open — legitimately in transit
Partial receipt; remaining quantity unclearInvestigate with the warehouse before excluding
Cost variance only; quantity is zeroExclude and post a JE for the dollar variance
  1. Before excluding, research each order in JD Edwards to confirm no further activity will occur.
  2. Check the ExclVarQty (Excluded Quantity Variance) and ExclVarAmt (Excluded Amount Variance) columns. Any non-zero value means new transactions have hit a previously excluded order — you must unexclude and re-exclude to recalculate.
  3. Note the total Exclusions amount at the bottom of the page. This becomes your offsetting journal entry amount in Step 6.
What is an exclusion?

Excluding an order pair tells RapidReconciler to stop counting it toward the In Transit balance. It does not change anything in JD Edwards — the GL balance still needs an offsetting journal entry.

Order pairs must be investigated in JD Edwards before they are excluded. Although an exclusion can be reversed at any time by unexcluding the order, exclusions made without first confirming the underlying cause can mask real issues — missing transactions, DMAAI configuration problems, or transfer cost variances that need to be corrected at the source.

Order Pair Columns to Know

ColumnMeaning
From / ToShipping and receiving branch plants
TranQtyIn-transit quantity (Ship Qty − Rec Qty)
TranAmtIn-transit amount (Ship Amt − Rec Amt)
Ship Sts / Rec StsCurrent status of the ST and OT orders
ExclQty / ExclAmtQuantity and amount previously excluded
ExclVarQty / ExclVarAmtNew activity on previously excluded orders — review every period

The Recalc Balance Button

RapidReconciler's data refreshes nightly, but exclusion changes you make on the Orders page do not need to wait for the next refresh cycle. Above the Orders grid (visible in the screenshot at the top of this page) is a Recalc Balance button.

When to use itWhat it does
After excluding or unexcluding one or more order pairs Immediately updates the In Transit Balance and variance calculations on the Reconciliation page so you can see the impact of your triage work without waiting for the nightly refresh.
Workflow

Complete all your exclusion decisions for the period first, then click Recalc Balance once. There is no need to recalc after every individual exclusion — batching them is faster and produces the same result.

Reminder

Recalc Balance only updates the calculations driven by Orders page activity (exclusions and the In Transit Balance). Journal entries posted in JD Edwards still require the next nightly refresh before they appear in RapidReconciler.

3 Step Three

Read the Reconciliation Page

Goal
Quantify the Out of Balance amount and decide what to investigate.

Procedure

  1. Return to the Reconciliation page.
  2. Look at the Valuation section:
GL Balance
F0902

Period-end balance of the In Transit clearing account

In Transit Balance
ST/OT Pairs

Calculated from open transfer activity (Shipments − Receipts)

Out of Balance
GL − In Transit

What needs to be explained and resolved

Decision

  1. If Out of Balance = $0, skip ahead to Step 7 — you are reconciled.
  2. If Out of Balance ≠ $0, continue to Step 4 to address each variance source.
4 Step Four

Resolve Each Variance Source

Goal
Walk down the Variance Calculation section and address every non-zero line. The sum of all lines equals the Out of Balance amount.

Variance Lines at a Glance

Variance LineMust Be Zero to Close?Resolution
Carry ForwardInvestigateResolve in prior period or roll into current JE
GL BatchesYesWork with finance to post all open batches
End of DayYesConfirm Sales Update (R42800) ran for transfer orders
TransactionsInvestigateDrill in via Step 5 and prepare offsetting JEs
ExclusionsPrepare JEPost journal entry for the excluded amount
Manual Journal EntriesInformationalConfirm entries are correctly reflected

Carry Forward

The unresolved out-of-balance amount from the prior period.

Resolve by: Returning to the prior period to investigate, or — if the prior period is closed — including the amount in the current period's manual journal entry. Document the reason.

GL Batches

Entries in F0911 where the posted code is not "P" (not yet posted to the GL).

Resolve by: Working with finance to post the outstanding batches. A bell icon flags batches more than 2 days old — prioritize these. If a batch header is missing, run JD Edwards' "Missing Batch Header" report.

Required

GL Batches must be zero before performing closing activities.

End of Day

Transfer sales orders that have been ship confirmed but not yet processed through Sales Update (R42800/P42800).

Resolve by: Confirming the Sales Update version configured for transfer orders is scheduled and completing successfully. The In Transit Sales Update version must have the A/R interface turned off. A bell icon flags orders more than 2 days old.

Required

End of Day must be zero before closing. Until Sales Update runs, the shipment has no batch number or GL date.

Transactions

Documents where the order lines (F4211 sales orders or F4311 purchase orders) do not match the GL (F0911). Common causes: DMAAI mismatches, fiscal period differences, account number discrepancies.

Resolve by: Going to the Transactions page (Step 5) to investigate each item. The corrective action is always an offsetting JE in JD Edwards plus a note in RapidReconciler.

Exclusions

The "leftover" balance from order pairs you excluded in Step 2.

Resolve by: Posting a journal entry in JD Edwards — debit or credit the In Transit account and offset to an appropriate variance or expense account per your organization's policy.

Manual Journal Entries

Manual entries posted directly to the In Transit GL account. Informational only — confirms they are reflected in the variance calculation.

Example Variance Calculation

Variance SourceAmountStatus
Carry Forward($750.10)Investigate prior period
GL Batches$0.00✓ Clear
End of Day$0.00✓ Clear
Transactions$0.00✓ Clear
Exclusions($23.31)Post journal entry
Manual Journal Entries$0.00✓ Clear
Unreconciled Variance($773.41)Total requiring resolution
5 Step Five

Investigate Transaction-Level Variances

Goal
Determine the root cause of every transaction variance and prepare corrective journal entries.

Procedure

  1. Navigate to the Transactions page.
  2. Note that transactions differing by less than 1 monetary unit are filtered by default but still counted in totals (administrator can adjust the tolerance).
  3. For each row, click the + icon on the left to open the Transaction Detail report. The report has three sections:
SectionContents
1 — F0911 GLAll F0911 rows for the company, document type, and document number
2 — Order LinesFor sales shipments, the F4211 sales order lines. For PO receipts, the F4311 purchase order lines.
3 — DMAAIsAll DMAAI entries for each GL class code (first row = model table)

Analysis Tips

  • Verify company number, account number, and period ending date match across Sections 1 and 2.
  • If account numbers differ, consult the DMAAI setup in Section 3.
  • Refer to the DMAAI Reference Guide for configuration help.
  1. Determine the root cause: DMAAI mismatch, period mismatch, or account mismatch.
  2. Prepare an offsetting journal entry in JD Edwards for each variance.
  3. Select the row on the Transactions page and click the Edit Note button to document the corrective action and (optionally) mark the row as worked.

The Edit Note Popup

Clicking Edit Note opens a popup that combines two actions in a single dialog:

FieldPurpose
Worked checkboxMarks the transaction as worked. Worked rows can be filtered off the grid using the Worked Transactions dropdown — but totals are not affected.
Note inputFree-text field for documenting the corrective action. Notes appear on the period-end audit report.
Best practice

Include your name and date in every note. Notes appear on the period-end audit report and become the audit trail for how each variance was resolved.

The Worked Transactions Filter

The Worked Transactions dropdown above the grid controls which rows are visible. It has three values:

ValueWhat It Shows
NoOnly transactions that have not yet been marked as worked. This is the working view during reconciliation — it leaves only what still needs attention.
YesOnly transactions that have been marked as worked. Useful for reviewing what's been documented and confirming notes are complete before close.
AllEvery transaction, regardless of worked status. Use this when reconciling totals or auditing.
Important

The Worked Transactions filter is a view filter only. It changes which rows appear on the grid but does not change the variance totals on the Reconciliation page. Marking a transaction worked does not resolve it — the underlying journal entry must still be posted in JD Edwards.

6 Step Six

Post Journal Entries and Close

Goal
Clear the remaining variance and confirm the period reconciles.

Pre-Close Checklist

    Confirm before posting
  • GL Batches = $0
  • End of Day = $0
  • All transaction variances reviewed and notes documented

Calculate the Closing Journal Entry Total

Once GL Batches and End of Day are at zero, the remaining Out of Balance is composed of three resolvable variance lines. The closing journal entry is a single combined entry that totals these three values to bring the Out of Balance to zero:

Closing JE Formula
Carry Forward  +  Transactions  +  Exclusions

The sum of all unresolved variances. Posting this entry should drive Out of Balance to $0.

ComponentWhat It Represents
Carry ForwardThe unresolved out-of-balance amount carried over from the prior period (when the prior period is closed and cannot be returned to).
TransactionsThe net total of all F4211 (sales order) or F4311 (purchase order) vs F0911 mismatches investigated in Step 5.
ExclusionsThe leftover balance from order pairs excluded in Step 2.
Why a single entry

Combining the three lines into one journal entry simplifies the audit trail and ensures the period closes cleanly. The Manual Journal Entries line on the Reconciliation page will reflect the posted total once the next nightly refresh runs, and the Out of Balance should be exactly zero.

Posting the Journal Entry

Use This ToolWhen
Journal Entry buttonOn the Reconciliation page — produces an Excel report of GL and In Transit balances to support account-level entries.
Suggested Entry popup (star icon, End of Day row)Generates a pre-built suggested journal entry for End of Day amounts at close. Powered by the offset accounts configured by an administrator.
Manual JE in JD EdwardsThe actual closing entry for Carry Forward + Transactions + Exclusions is posted manually in JD Edwards. See the Transfer Order Exclusion Guide for guidance on the exclusion portion.

The Suggested Entry Popup

Clicking the star icon on the End of Day row in the Variance Calculation section opens the RR Suggested Entry popup. The popup is pre-built using the offset accounts configured by an administrator (see the Administrator Walkthrough → Offset Accounts) and gives you a head start on the closing journal entry.

Reading the popup

ColumnDescription
CompanyNumberThe company the entry is for. Multi-company environments produce multiple paired rows in a single popup.
PeriodEndsThe period the entry should be posted to.
LongAccountThe In Transit clearing GL account being cleared.
SEQSequence within the entry. SEQ 0 is the In Transit side; SEQ 1 is the offset side. The two amounts net to zero per pair.
JE_AccountThe GL account for that line. SEQ 0 always uses the In Transit account itself. SEQ 1 uses the offset account configured by the administrator for the matching Company / DocType / OrderType combination.
JE_AmountThe dollar amount for that line. Negatives clear the In Transit balance; positives post to the offset account.

Working with the entry

  1. Click Export to download the entry as Excel.
  2. Review the JE_Account column. If any rows show a generic placeholder (such as COGS, Tolerance Adjust, or TBD), replace them with the correct GL accounts per your internal procedures. The freeform field on the offset accounts setup intentionally allows generic labels — they are placeholders waiting for resolution at close.
  3. Copy the two rightmost columns (JE_Account and JE_Amount) into your JD Edwards journal entry template.
  4. Post the JE in JDE.
End of Day only — close-only tool

The Suggested Entry popup is for the End of Day variance source only. There is no equivalent for the Transactions row in this module — those variances are handled through individual Edit Note entries and a manual JE. Also do not use the Suggested Entry popup mid-period — the offset accounts are configured to clear what's open at close, and using it before close can produce an incomplete entry.

Confirm Reconciliation

After posting in JD Edwards:

  1. Wait for the next nightly refresh — RapidReconciler does not see your JE until then.
  2. Return to the Reconciliation page.
  3. Confirm Out of Balance = $0.
  4. If a residual variance remains, return to Step 4 and identify which line moved.
7 Step Seven

Run Integrity Reports and Save the Audit Report

Goal
Verify clean configuration and produce permanent documentation.

Run the Four Integrity Reports

ReportNamePurpose
1Model AAI TableVerify all GL class codes have correct account assignments
2Missing GL ClassesAdd missing GL class codes to the transit DMAAI table 4310
3Order ExclusionsConfirm all exclusions are correct and accounted for
4Transfer Cost VariancesInvestigate any orders with non-zero UnitPrVar — most common cause of unresolved In Transit balances

Save the Audit Report

  1. Click the Audit Report button.
  2. Save the output (Excel or PDF) to a dedicated period-end folder.
Important

Save the Audit Report before any data purge. Detail data may be removed during a purge and cannot be recreated.

What the Audit Report Contains

SectionContents
Accounts SummaryValuation and variance summary per account
Unposted GL BatchesDetails of any remaining unposted batches
End of DaySales orders awaiting Sales Update
Manual Journal EntriesAll manual entries to the account
VariancesTransaction variances with user-entered notes
Perpetual DetailsItem balances and values at period end
You're done

The period is reconciled and documented.

Reference — Key Pages and What They Show

The four primary pages in the In Transit module and how they behave with the period selector.

PageShowsPeriod Behavior
ReconciliationValuation, variance calculation, and overall close statusHonors selected period
OrdersOpen ST/OT order pairs with a remaining quantity or amountAlways current position regardless of period
TransactionsF4211 / F4311 vs F0911 mismatches at the document levelHonors selected period
As-OfIn-transit position as of any historical period endCalculated backwards from the Orders page totals

About the As-Of Page

The As-Of page reconstructs what was in transit at any prior period end by walking backward from the current Orders page totals. The result is a complete, item-level snapshot showing what goods were sitting in the In Transit clearing account at the close of the selected period — useful for audit support, period comparisons, and month-end review.

Behavior

  • The total will always match the Reconciliation page total for the same period.
  • It will only match the Orders page total if the current period is selected.
  • Calculations are dynamic — backdated activity changes prior As-Of positions.
  • "Exclusion Adjust" entries in detail are RapidReconciler adjustments, not JD Edwards transactions.

Expanding a Row — The In Transit Cardex

Clicking the + icon at the left of any row expands an inline sub-grid that acts as a cardex for In Transit — the full transaction history for that single item, walked backward in date sequence from the selected period end. This is the most powerful feature of the As-Of page.

The first row of the expansion shows the period-end balance ("End bal"). Each subsequent row is a transaction that affected the in-transit position for that item, ordered most-recent-first. Together, they tell the story of how that item's in-transit balance was built up over time.

Sub-grid columns

ColumnDescription
companyJD Edwards company number associated with the transaction.
longaccountThe In Transit clearing GL account.
branchThe branch plant on the transaction (typically the shipping or receiving branch).
itemnumberThe item being transferred — same item across all rows of the expansion.
lotLot number, when the item is lot-controlled.
glclassThe GL class code on the transaction, used to map into the DMAAI tables.
period end dateThe period the transaction is recorded against.
creation dateThe date the transaction was created in JD Edwards. Differences between this and the period end date indicate backdated activity.
commentsNotes on the row — for example, the first row of the expansion is labeled "End bal" to indicate the period-ending balance.

Source data

The transaction detail in the expansion is sourced from sales and purchase orders (F4211 / F4311) — not the item ledger (F4111). This is consistent with how the rest of the In Transit module operates, since transfers are recorded via ST/OT order pairs rather than direct cardex entries.

Exporting the As-Of Position

The As-Of page — including expanded rows — can be exported to Excel for offline analysis, audit packages, or comparison against external reports. The exported file contains more detail than the in-app expansion shows on screen, with several additional columns useful for deeper investigation.

Additional columns in the export

Beyond the columns visible in the in-app expansion, the Excel export includes several columns that support deeper analysis:

ColumnDescription
runqtyRunning quantity. The cumulative in-transit quantity for the item as of each row, walked backward from the period-end balance.
runamtRunning amount. The cumulative in-transit dollar value for the item, walked backward in the same way.
dtDocument type code on the transaction (for example, TR for transfer receipts, OW for transfer shipments).
docJD Edwards document number for the transaction.
qtyTransaction quantity — the quantity moved by this single transaction (positive for receipts, negative for shipments).
uomUnit of measure for the transaction quantity.
valTransaction value — the dollar amount of this single transaction.
ordnumOrder number — the JDE order this transaction belongs to (the ST or OT order number).
ordtypeOrder type for ordnum — typically ST (transfer sales) or OT (transfer purchase).
relnumRelated order number — the matched pair on the other side of the transfer.
reltypeRelated order type for relnum.
Why the running columns matter

The runqty and runamt columns make it easy to spot the exact moment an item went in or out of an unbalanced state. Sorting or scanning the column reveals when the in-transit balance jumped, plateaued, or began drifting — without having to manually add up individual transactions.

Worked Example

When the cardex tells the real story

The export above is a real-world example. The item shows an End bal runamt of -366.34, which an analyst would naturally want to clear before period-end close. The expansion includes an Exclusion Adjust row, which is the most visually distinctive entry in the cardex — and a tempting first place to look.

The intuitive (but wrong) read

Looking at the expansion, the eye is drawn to the Exclusion Adjust row at 2015-10-03. The natural conclusion: someone excluded an order pair, the exclusion is responsible for the leftover variance, and reversing it would bring End bal back to zero.

What the math actually shows

Walking the chronology forward from Beg bal (the bottom of the export) and summing every val, the running balance arrives at exactly -366.34 by the time we reach End bal. So the dollar variance is real — but here's the catch:

RowperiodendsvalEffect
Exclusion Adjust (ST 24473 / OT 23868)2015-10-030.00No dollar impact

The Exclusion Adjust has val = 0.00. Reversing it would not change End bal at all. The exclusion is a marker, not a debit or credit.

Furthermore, the order pair the exclusion applies to (ST 24473 / OT 23868) is fully balanced when its three rows are summed: +956.02 shipment + 0.00 receipt + -956.02 shipment = $0.00. There was nothing wrong with that pair — the exclusion was a misdiagnosis.

The real culprit

Grouping all transactions by order pair (using the ordnum and relnum columns) reveals exactly one pair that does not net to zero:

Order PairSideBranchqtyval
OT 23885 / ST 24491OT receipt (IDM)—-480-366.34
ST shipment (CBT)—+4800.00
Net0-366.34

The quantities net to zero (480 in, 480 out — the goods actually moved correctly), but the dollars don't: the receipt posted at $366.34 while the shipment posted at $0.00. This is a classic UnitPrVar — a transfer cost variance arising when the ST sales price doesn't equal the OT purchase cost. It's exactly what Integrity Report 4 is designed to surface, and it has no relationship to the Exclusion Adjust on the other pair.

Takeaway
  • The Exclusion Adjust is unnecessary — it was applied to a pair that was already balanced. Reverse it to clean up the cardex, but doing so will not change End bal.
  • The real fix is on OT 23885 / ST 24491 — investigate why the shipment posted at $0 cost. Likely DMAAI 4335 configuration or a misaligned ST/OT cost. Correct in JD Edwards.
  • Without the export, neither finding is obvious. The in-app expansion shows the Exclusion Adjust prominently but doesn't reveal which order pair is actually unbalanced. The val, ordnum, and relnum columns in the export are what makes the diagnosis possible.
Common uses

The As-Of page and its export are particularly useful for:

  • Investigating stuck balances — expand any row carrying an unexpected balance to trace the transaction history that produced it.
  • Backdating analysis — comparing creationdate to periodends highlights transactions that posted to a period after the fact.
  • Order pair matching — using the ordnum / relnum columns to confirm that ST and OT sides reconcile.
  • Period-over-period comparison — pick two prior dates, export each, and compare to identify what changed.
  • Audit documentation — exported snapshots provide a reproducible record of the In Transit position at any historical point.
Note

Since calculations are dynamic, exporting the As-Of position immediately after period close gives the cleanest reference snapshot. If transactions are later backdated into the period, a fresh export will reflect those changes.

Reference — Variance Sources and Resolution Actions

Each source of variance, where it originates, where it is resolved, and whether it must be zero before closing.

SourceWhere It Comes FromWhere to ResolveMust Be Zero to Close?
Carry ForwardPrior period out-of-balancePrior period or current JEInvestigate
GL BatchesF0911 unposted entriesFinance — post the batchesYes
End of DayShip confirmed without Sales UpdateR42800 / P42800Yes
TransactionsF4211 / F4311 vs F0911 mismatchJE in JDE + note in RapidReconcilerInvestigate
ExclusionsExcluded order pair leftoversJE in JDEPrepare JE
Manual JEsDirect GL entriesVerify onlyInformational

Reference — Integrity Reports

Review these when RapidReconciler is first installed and monthly during period-end close.

ReportNameFrequencyPurpose
0JDE AAIsDebugging onlyAnalyze DMAAI configuration when investigating Transactions items
1Model AAI TableBefore GL adjustmentsDMAAI 4152 entries for transfer order GL account assignment
2Missing GL ClassesMonthlyTransfer pairs where GL class code is missing from DMAAI 4310 — silently excluded otherwise
3Order ExclusionsMonthlyAll excluded order pairs — verify exclusions and confirm audit trail
4Transfer Cost VariancesMonthlyOrders where ST sales cost/price ≠ OT purchase cost — most common cause of stuck In Transit balances

Report 4 — Key Columns

ColumnDescription
TransPrcTransfer cost or price of the item
SOExtCost / SOExtAmtExtended cost and price on the sales order
POUnitCost / POTotalUnit cost and total on the purchase order
UnitPrVarVariance between sales price and purchase cost — amount that will not clear
QtyVarDifference in units shipped vs received
CostMthdCost method in receiving branch — determines variance handling
Watch this column

A non-zero UnitPrVar means the In Transit account will carry a balance for that order pair even after receipt. This is the most common cause of unresolved In Transit variances. Review DMAAI 4335 configuration and ST/OT price management procedures.

Reference — Supporting Widgets and Tools

The supporting widgets available across the In Transit module and what each one is for. Most of them live on the Reconciliation page and surface the data you'll use during the seven-step process.

ToolWhere FoundWhat It Does
Drill Down WidgetReconciliation pageVisual breakdown of where the largest variances exist; drill from currency → company → BU → account
Suggested Entry PopupStar icon on End of Day rowGenerates a pre-built suggested journal entry for End of Day amounts at close (covered in Step 6)
Out of Balance History GraphReconciliation page14-period trend; click a data point to jump to that period
Journal Entry ButtonReconciliation pageExcel report of GL and In Transit balances for account-level JEs
Audit Report ButtonReconciliation pageFull period-end documentation in Excel or PDF

The Drill Down Widget Up Close

The Drill Down widget is the most useful of the five tools for diagnosis work. At the top level it shows the variance distribution by base currency. Clicking a slice drills one level deeper: currency → company → business unit → account. At each level, the donut splits into the slices that exist at that level, and a small left-arrow appears in the header to navigate back up.

How to use it during reconciliation

  1. Start at the top level — confirm which base currency holds the variance (in single-currency environments this is a single solid donut).
  2. Drill into the currency to see the breakdown by company. The largest slice is usually where the investigation should start.
  3. Drill into a company to see business units, then drill into a BU to see accounts.
  4. Use the back-arrow in the header at any time to return to the previous level.
When this widget shines

The Drill Down is most valuable in multi-company or multi-BU environments where the Out of Balance is a roll-up across many entities. It surfaces the largest contributor visually so you don't have to filter through the Transactions or Orders pages to find it. In single-company / single-BU environments the widget still works but adds less value than other tools.

Troubleshooting Common Scenarios

Common issues encountered during reconciliation and how to resolve them.

"The In Transit Validation light is red."

A prior-period issue is affecting carry forward. Hover for details, navigate to the prior period, and resolve the residual variance before continuing.

"GL Batches won't go to zero."

A batch is unposted in JD Edwards. Look for the bell icon flagging batches over 2 days old. If a batch header is missing, run the JDE "Missing Batch Header" report.

"End of Day amount keeps growing."

Sales Update (R42800) is not running for transfer orders. Confirm the version is scheduled, completing successfully, and configured with the A/R interface off.

"I excluded an order, and now its ExclVarQty is non-zero."

A new transaction hit the previously excluded order. Unexclude and re-exclude to recalculate the exclusion amount — do not ignore it.

"The Out of Balance is small but won't clear."

Check the tolerance setting on the Transactions page. Sub-tolerance amounts are excluded from the grid display but still counted in the variance total. Drill into Report 4 — a small UnitPrVar on multiple orders may explain the residual.

"I posted a JE in JDE but RapidReconciler still shows the variance."

Data refreshes nightly. Wait until tomorrow's refresh and re-check.

"The As-Of page total doesn't match the Orders page."

This is expected unless the current period is selected. As-Of reflects historical positions; Orders always reflects the current position.

"I can only see 14 periods of history."

That's the default retention. Contact your administrator to request a purge or extension.

Best Practices and Tips

Habits that make the reconciliation easier, faster, and more reliable over time.

  • Reconcile weekly, not just at month-end. Catching a variance the day after it occurs is dramatically easier than untangling a month of accumulated variances.
  • Always investigate before excluding. An unjustified exclusion hides the real problem and can mask DMAAI configuration issues.
  • Document every transaction note with your name and date. It saves your future self (and the auditor) significant time.
  • Run the Audit Report every period and store it in a dedicated folder. Detail data may be lost in a purge.
  • Treat Integrity Report 4 as an early warning system. Cost variances surface here before they accumulate into period-end pain.
  • Keep the In Transit Sales Update version separate from regular Sales Update. It must have the A/R interface turned off.
  • Coordinate with finance on GL Batches early in the close. Posting batches is often the longest pole in the tent.
  • Use the Drill Down Widget in multi-company environments to identify which entity is driving the variance before diving into transactions.

Recommended Cadence

How often to perform each part of the reconciliation work.

ActivityFrequency
Review Orders page; process exclusionsDaily or weekly — never wait until period end
Confirm Sales Update has run for transfer ordersDaily
Full reconciliation reviewWeekly
Integrity Reports 1–4Monthly (period end)
Closing activities and Audit ReportPeriod end
Configuration review (Report 0, DMAAI setup)At install + when issues arise

Glossary

Key terms used throughout this guide.

TermDefinition
DMAAIDistribution / Manufacturing Automatic Accounting Instructions — JDE table that maps transactions to GL accounts
F0902JDE Account Balances table (GL period balances)
F0911JDE Account Ledger table (GL detail)
F4111JDE Item Ledger (Cardex) table
F4211JDE Sales Order Detail table — contains the flag identifying transfer types
In Transit Clearing AccountGL account that temporarily holds the value of goods shipped but not yet received
Order PairSummarized view of a transfer at the ST sales order, OT purchase order, and item number level
OT OrderTransfer purchase order (receiving side)
R42800 / P42800JDE Sales Update — the program that posts ST orders to the GL
ST OrderTransfer sales order (shipping side)
Status 999JDE order status indicating the order is fully closed and no further activity is possible
UnitPrVarPer-unit price variance between ST sales price and OT purchase cost
